Generate Random Samples from a Dirichlet Distribution

Source: R/rdirichlet.R
rdirichlet.Rd

Generates random samples from a Dirichlet distribution using the Gamma representation: if \(Y_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_i, 1)\) independently, then \((Y_1/S, \ldots, Y_k/S) \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_k)\), where \(S = \sum_i Y_i\).

Usage

rdirichlet(n, alpha)

Arguments

n

A positive integer specifying the number of random vectors to generate.

alpha

A numeric vector of length >= 2 containing positive concentration parameters of the Dirichlet distribution. All elements must be strictly positive.

Value

A numeric matrix of dimensions n x length(alpha) when n > 1, or a numeric vector of length length(alpha) when n = 1. Each row is one random draw from the Dirichlet distribution, sums to 1, and has all elements in [0, 1].

Details

The Dirichlet distribution is a multivariate generalisation of the Beta distribution and is commonly used as a conjugate prior for multinomial proportions in Bayesian statistics.

The probability density function is: $$f(x_1, \ldots, x_k) = \frac{\Gamma(\sum_i \alpha_i)}{\prod_i \Gamma(\alpha_i)} \prod_i x_i^{\alpha_i - 1}$$ where \(x_i > 0\) and \(\sum_i x_i = 1\).

Key properties:

  • Each marginal follows a Beta distribution: \(X_i \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_i,\, \sum_{j \neq i} \alpha_j)\).

  • \(E[X_i] = \alpha_i / \sum_j \alpha_j\).

  • Components are negatively correlated unless one component dominates.

Implementation steps:

  1. Generate independent \(Y_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_i, 1)\) for each \(i = 1, \ldots, k\).

  2. Normalise: \(X_i = Y_i / \sum_j Y_j\).

Examples

# Example 1: Generate 5 samples from Dirichlet(1, 1, 1) - uniform on simplex
samples <- rdirichlet(5, c(1, 1, 1))
print(samples)
#>             [,1]      [,2]       [,3]
#> [1,] 0.200996244 0.5271029 0.27190090
#> [2,] 0.281677187 0.3391950 0.37912784
#> [3,] 0.002984537 0.1840926 0.81292285
#> [4,] 0.464745472 0.5161844 0.01907013
#> [5,] 0.023603645 0.4488256 0.52757077
rowSums(samples)  # Each row should sum to 1
#> [1] 1 1 1 1 1

# Example 2: Generate samples with unequal concentrations
samples <- rdirichlet(1000, c(2, 5, 3))
colMeans(samples)  # Expected values: approximately c(0.2, 0.5, 0.3)
#> [1] 0.1953453 0.5070255 0.2976292

# Example 3: Sparse Dirichlet (small alpha values)
samples <- rdirichlet(100, c(0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1))
head(samples)  # Most weight concentrated on one component
#>              [,1]         [,2]         [,3]         [,4]
#> [1,] 5.282956e-04 0.9577091874 2.114759e-18 4.176252e-02
#> [2,] 1.502695e-03 0.0022626805 3.937413e-06 9.962307e-01
#> [3,] 3.216073e-01 0.0077025856 2.170034e-06 6.706879e-01
#> [4,] 1.911701e-18 0.0289739529 7.924093e-01 1.786167e-01
#> [5,] 8.743134e-02 0.0006080105 9.119606e-01 2.922731e-10
#> [6,] 9.224319e-01 0.0775681023 5.608013e-10 1.911638e-13

# Example 4: Concentrated Dirichlet (large alpha values)
samples <- rdirichlet(100, c(100, 100, 100))
colMeans(samples)  # Concentrated around c(1/3, 1/3, 1/3)
#> [1] 0.3324375 0.3346551 0.3329074

# Example 5: Bayesian update with Jeffreys prior for 4 categories
prior_alpha     <- c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5)
observed_counts <- c(10, 5, 8, 7)
posterior_samples <- rdirichlet(1000, prior_alpha + observed_counts)
colMeans(posterior_samples)  # Posterior mean
#> [1] 0.3303519 0.1704897 0.2674610 0.2316974
